CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar May-08-2025 Set For Trial On Jul-20-26 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

Petition For Leave To File Civil Action
Off Calendar Mar-24-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: RE: Plaintiff ELLEN RAYZ', AN INDIVIDUAL, Petition For Leave To File Civil Action.

No Appearances. The court adopts its tentative ruling. Plaintiff Ellen Rayz's "Petition for Leave to File Civil Action" is granted.

Rayz worked for the San Francisco Community College District as Chief Technology Officer from 2020, until her March 25, 2024, resignation. On August 23, 2024, Rayz presented a tort claim form to the District based on sex, gender, and national origin workplace discrimination, harassment, and retaliation. On October 4, 2024, the District issued a letter rejecting Rayz's tort claim form, maintaining it was not presented within the six-month period prescribed by the Government Claims Act (the Act). On November 5, 2024, the District denied Rayz's application to file a late tort claim. Rayz sued the District in December 2024, and now petitions for leave to file a late claim under Government Code 946.6, solely as to her eleventh cause of action for retaliation in violation of Labor Code sections 98.6 and 1102.5.

Under Government Code 911.2, a claimant must present a personal injury claim no later than six months after the cause of action accrues. "The date of accrual for purposes of the claim presentation requirement is the same date on which the cause of action would accrue for purposes of the statute of limitations in an action against a private party." (Ovando v. Cnty. of Los Angeles (2008)159 Cal. App. 4th 42, 62-63 [internal citations and quotations omitted].) "Under the continuous violation doctrine, a tort cause of action accrues when the last wrongful act or injury occurs, effectively delaying the running of the statute of limitations until that point." (Willis v. City of Carlsbad (2020) 48 Cal.App.5th 1104.)

Here, Rayz alleges her supervisor retaliated against her after she circulated two internal complaints of his allegedly discriminatory and harassing conduct. Rayz alleges his bullying intensified after she filed the initial complaint on February 7, 2024. She describes his conduct as "continuing and in fact, increasingly derogatory." Rayz alleges the District ratified this conduct by doing nothing in response to the initial complaint. This prompted Rayz to file a second complaint on March 12, 2024, disclosing "she had no alternative but to resign." These allegations describe continuous retaliatory conduct and ratification occurring well into March 2024. Thus, under the continuous violations doctrine, Rayz's 8/23/24 claim presentation was timely.

The court believes that this petition was filed out of "abundance of caution" if it was determined Rayz did not comply with the Act's claim presentation requirements. In the same spirit of "abundance of caution" shown by the filing of this petition, the court also grants the petition as an independent and alternative decision. Even if Rayz presented her tort claim form more than six months past the date her retaliation claim accrued, the court finds she has met all the requirements set forth in Government Code 946.6 for the relief she seeks. Specifically, any failure by Rayz to present the claim sooner was through mistake, inadvertence and/or excusable neglect. Further, the District has not provided evidence of any particular prejudice by the granting of the relief that Rayz seeks. (DeVore v. Dept. of California Highway Patrol (2013) 221 Cal.App.4th 454, 459 ["In the absence of any evidence of particular prejudice to the public entity, a trial court abuses its discretion in denying a petition" per section 946.6].)

Judge: Harold Kahn. Clerk: R. Woods. Not Reported. (302/HK)

***After hearing: An order confirming the tentative ruling is executed.
